one_line_takeaway: Dense-retriever RAG in medical/legal Q&A is universally poisonable by naively concatenating any payload onto a known query - an 'orthogonal augmentation' effect preserves query-document similarity - and a shrinkage-Mahalanobis detector on embeddings reliably catches such documents where L2-norm defenses fail.
key_finding: Dense retrieval in medical RAG is universally vulnerable: high top-2 attack success rates hold across essentially all corpus/retriever/query/payload combinations, and are largely INDEPENDENT of the payload type (since none is semantically aligned with the query), of the corpus, and of the retriever. Success is highest for long queries (PubMedQA, MedQA reach ~0.98-1.0) and lower for shorter-query sets like MedMCQA/BioASQ under Contriever (~0.6). The attack survives query paraphrasing (top-2 success ~0.8 in most cases) and works with attacker-generated synthetic doctor queries (~0.92 for Contriever+PubMed), so the attacker only needs approximate knowledge of query phrasing. Mechanistically, clean retrieved documents are surprisingly dissimilar to their queries (Contriever query-vs-retrieved angle ~70 degrees; inner product <25% of query-self inner product), leaving a gap that near-orthogonal poisoned documents exploit. The proposed Mahalanobis-distance defense achieves near-perfect detection in almost all cases where the standard L2-norm defense fails, and also filters >95% of PoisonedRAG [15]-style attacks. (The L2-norm defense does work for Contriever, attributed to Contriever's sensitivity to document length.)
core_contribution: The paper studies the adversarial robustness of the RETRIEVAL component of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in knowledge-intensive domains (primarily medical Q&A, with a legal Q&A case study). It makes three of its own contributions: (1) It demonstrates 'universal poisoning attacks' against dense retrievers, in which an adversary appends arbitrary targeted information (PII, adversarial treatment recommendations, jailbreak prompts) to attacker-known queries and injects the resulting document into the corpus, causing it to be retrieved at high rank whenever the corresponding query is issued. This is shown across 225 combinations of corpus/retriever/query/targeted-information. (2) It offers a mechanistic explanation via an 'orthogonal augmentation property' of dense retrievers: when a document p is near-orthogonal (in embedding) to a query q, the embedding shift from f(q) to f([q+p]) is roughly perpendicular to f(q), so the query-poisoned-document similarity is approximately preserved at the query-self similarity level; combined with the empirical finding that clean retrieved documents are themselves only weakly similar to their queries (~70 degrees angle for Contriever), this explains why poisoned documents outrank clean ones. (3) It proposes a detection-based defense using a shrinkage-regularized Mahalanobis distance over embeddings (against an anchor set of clean documents), which separates poisoned from clean documents where the L2-norm defense fails. NOTE: PoisonedRAG [15] is CITED prior work, not this paper's contribution; the authors reuse its adversarial passages as one type of targeted information and explicitly distinguish their goal (probing retriever robustness to arbitrary content in healthcare) from PoisonedRAG's goal (injecting query-relevant context to steer the LLM's generation in general Q&A).
threat_model: Attacker capabilities (Section 2): (1) Corpus access - the attacker can INJECT new entries into the data corpus (e.g., create a new Wikipedia page, add a fake patient row to a medical database, or exploit publicly writable sources like PubMed), but cannot modify existing clean documents. (2) Retriever access - the attacker can query the retriever and observe returned documents and their latent embeddings (query/embedding oracle), but CANNOT read or modify the retriever's parameters (black-box on parameters). (3) Query access - the attacker knows the queries of interest, OR (a more practical relaxation examined in the paper) only their semantically-equivalent/paraphrased versions. Attacker goal: ensure a targeted document T is consistently retrieved at high rank (top-K) for the attacker-specified query set Q after injection. Downstream harms: PII leakage, adversarial treatment recommendations, and jailbreaking the LLM once the poisoned document is used as context. The paper argues the model is realistic because public biomedical corpora (PubMed) are commonly used to build medical RAG by NIH/Mayo Clinic researchers.
limitations: (1) The attack requires the attacker to know the target queries at least up to paraphrase; the paper argues real medical queries follow standard patterns, but effectiveness still depends on normal users actually issuing attacker-anticipated queries. (2) Only dense retrievers are attacked (Contriever, SPECTER, MedCPT); sparse retrievers like BM25 are discussed only as generally weaker, not attacked. (3) The PubMed corpus used is a random subset (~2M of the full 23M documents) due to compute limits, so scale effects on retrieval ranking are not fully characterized. (4) The defense assumes the defender has full retriever access AND a clean anchor set of documents tied to the queries to protect - a fairly strong assumption; if the anchor set is itself contaminated or the protected queries are unknown, applicability is unclear. (5) The end-to-end LLM harm (actual PII leakage / jailbreak / wrong treatment in generation) is motivated and illustrated but the quantitative evaluation centers on RETRIEVAL success, not downstream generation success rates. (6) The orthogonal augmentation property is an empirical/heuristic argument with approximations, not a formal guarantee.

datasets_used: Query sets (medical): MMLU-Med (1089 entries), MedQA-US (1273), MedMCQA (4183), PubMedQA (500), BioASQ-Y/N (618)
Medical corpora: Textbook (~126K documents), StatPearls (~301K), PubMed (~2M-document random subset of the full 23M)
Legal: Australian Open Legal QA / Open Australian Legal Corpus (ALQA) used with CBR-RAG

Targeted-information payloads: synthetic PII, synthetic medical diagnosis, and adversarial passages from PoisonedRAG for MS-MARCO, Natural Questions (NQ), and HotpotQA; plus two synthetic legal payloads for the legal case
quantitative_results: 225 total corpus x retriever x query x targeted-information combinations evaluated for medical Q&A; std of success rates < 0.07

Table 1 top-2 retrieval success: MedQA reaches 1.0 with Contriever across all corpora; PubMedQA and MedQA under MedCPT ~0.97-0.99; short-query sets like MedMCQA/BioASQ under Contriever drop to ~0.58-0.68; illustrative top-left cell = 0.78 (Textbook, MedCPT, MMLU-Med, PII)
Attack robust to GPT-4 query paraphrasing: top-2 success ~0.8 in most cases
GPT-4-generated synthetic doctor queries (MedMCQA-based, 100 queries): ~0.92 average success for Contriever on PubMed
Legal Q&A (Table 2) top-2 success: Contriever 0.81-0.88, SPECTER 0.78-0.92 across PII/HotpotQA/NQ/two synthetic legal payloads
Orthogonal augmentation (Table 3): as f(q)^T f(p) decreases, angle of [f([q+p])-f(q)] vs f(q) approaches ~90 deg (Contriever: 99.1->92.5 deg; MedCPT: ~96-99 deg)
Clean-retrieval gap (Table 4): Contriever query-vs-5NN-retrieved angle ~69-72 deg, inner product <25% of query-self inner product; MedCPT angle ~41-53 deg
Defense: threshold at 95th quantile of anchor scores yields near-perfect detection in almost all cases; >95% filtering rate applied to PoisonedRAG [15]-style attacks
baselines_compared: L2-norm-of-embedding defense (standard poisoning detector; ineffective except for Contriever, where it works due to Contriever's document-length sensitivity)
L2 (Euclidean) distance / isotropic assumption (fails to separate clean vs poisoned)
Retrievers benchmarked as attack surfaces: Contriever, SPECTER, MedCPT
PoisonedRAG [15] - cited concurrent attack, used both as a payload source and as a target attack against which the proposed defense is evaluated (distinct goal from this paper)
